{"step_by_step_thinking": "Cadherins are a family of transmembrane proteins that play a key role in cell-cell adhesion. Multiple documents confirm that cadherins are integral to cell adhesion mechanisms. For example, Document [7] states that cadherins mediate cell-to-cell contact and are involved in contact inhibition, with E-cadherin (a type of cadherin) binding to β-catenin to regulate cell morphology and organization. Document [15] describes adherens junctions, which use cadherin to influence epithelial cell shape and structure. Document [14] mentions cadherin-1 as a predicted biomarker in a prognostic model, indicating its relevance in tissue context. Additionally, Document [24] notes that the RET protein has a cadherin-like domain, further supporting the structural and functional role of cadherin-like domains in cell adhesion. Since cadherins are transmembrane proteins, they are inherently located in the plasma membrane. Therefore, cadherin is a plasma membrane marker.", "answer_choice": "A"}
